prompt: What’s the current take on isometric training for maintaining strength on squat, bench, and deadlift when equipment is limited?
expected_output: Greg said isometrics can work, but the most effective style appears to be high-intent, ballistic maximal contractions (trying to hit max force as fast as possible), which can be hard to set up in lockdown conditions. A gym example he gave was pulling maximally into safety pins at a chosen deadlift height. He noted strong angle specificity: gains transfer most to roughly the trained joint position, about 15-30 degrees in either direction. That means isometric quarter-squat work won’t strongly improve bottom-position squat strength, while isometrics near a sticking point are more likely to help there. He also mentioned isometrics can be a useful complement since they avoid eccentric loading and can carry a different fatigue profile. But he cautioned that practical programming prescriptions (optimal set duration, frequency, and weekly dose) are still less standardized than conventional training.
assertions: Answer captures the core SBS position on isometric strength training
Answer includes key specific details, numbers, or mechanisms mentioned by the hosts
Answer acknowledges important caveats or limitations discussed by the hosts
Answer includes actionable practical recommendations consistent with the hosts' advice
Answer does not contradict the hosts' stated position
